responseSet.title: The Biological Aspect of Freudian Psycho-Analysis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Biological Aspect of Freudian Psycho-Analysis11

R. Brun

R. Brun

R. Brun  

In the past ten years, movements seem to have gained ground in Freudian analysis which deviate greatly in important points from Freud's original theories. These new movements, in my opinion, can no longer lay claim to the name psycho-analysis. There are, admittedly, various stages of transition, from slight deviations to wide fundamental differences. No great objection, for example, could be put forward concerning the so-called 'neo-analysis' of Schultz-Hencke, if, as its author maintains, it were concerned with mere differences in terminology. Experience warns us, however, that the abandonment of a scientific terminology almost inevitably leads, slowly but surely, to falsification, and eventually to the disintegration of the subject-matter. Usually it is the first step which signalizes the ruin of the whole system.

Rather more harmless, in comparison, seems the movement whose tendency it is to look upon and practise psycho-analysis from the lofty standpoint of a religious orientation. There is not much to be said against this so long as its proponents employ proper psycho-analytic technique in their work, recognizing the theories and not infringing them, with the purpose of combining the fruits of analysis with spiritual guidance and religious influence. It makes little difference whether we interpret 'morality' purely biologically as the sum of all the inhibitions imposed by the social environment, or whether we regard it as the categorical imperative of a transcendent being to which the human being has to answer. Both alternatives concern the same play of energies between drive and inhibition, the latter originating from denials and usually leading to frustration.

Those 'deep psychological' tendencies, however, which result in a so-called 'existentialist analysis' derived from the existentialist philosophy of Heidegger (L. Binswanger, M. Boss, and others), and which do not stop at mere deviations in terminology or philosophical extensions of Freudian theory, seem to us in principle incompatible with Freudian psycho-analysis; here we are faced rather with a farreaching transformation of the scientific principles of psycho-analysis.

responseSet.title: Variation on a Theme
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Variation on a Theme

Nancy Procter-Gregg

Nancy Procter-Gregg

Nancy Procter-Gregg
LONDON

Nancy Procter-Gregg  

W. S. Gilbert professed dislike of Shakespeare, and was particularly irritated by Hamlet. The latter indeed was his King Charles's Head, cropping up through allusion and analogy in unexpected places throughout his works, and responsible incidentally for one of his jokes which has passed into our speech, when he said to Tree. of his performance that he 'never saw anything so funny in my life, and yet it was not in the least vulgar'. He made two determined direct attempts to dismiss the haunting theme, in the skit Rosencrantz and Guildenstern, and the comic opera The Mountebanks. In the former, which reads drearily to-day and can hardly have been entirely redeemed by the author's gusto when taking part in it in private theatricals, Ophelia vents Gilbert's impatience with Hamlet's soliloquies and magniloquence, and the English reverence for the play, by tricking him into presenting a 'damned' tragedy written long before by Claudius but received as a farce; in penalty for which he is banished to be a bore in England, and Ophelia is free to marry Rosencrantz. The Mountebanks exhibit Hamlet and Ophelia as their prize clockwork figures, and when by magic two real characters are transformed into the automatons they regard it as great nonsense that the girl should have to commit suicide because her lover will not marry her when, after all, he wishes to.

These unsatisfactory attempts to dispose of the matter illustrate merely the exemplification in Gilbert of a psychological truism—he, too, 'protests too much', and shows affinity by his preoccupation. But in another, and a very fine, work he hit on the humorous approach to the theme of guilt tragically embodied in Oedipus Rex, Hamlet, and The Brothers Karamazov, in the progression traced by Freud; and happened to stage neatly a dramatization of the latter's technical theory of humour itself.1

Of all the Gilbert and Sullivan operas, Ruddigore 'dates' least, and from the time of the first post-war generation it has grown in estimation. Book and score each show their composers at their best, and the marriage of talents is here conspicuously successful. The libretto sparkles with wit, and the music shines with feeling; as their biographer Hesketh Pearson acutely observes, Sullivan's view that there is no such thing as humour in music was an important factor for the success of his collaboration with Gilbert. We may alter the emphasis of the further conclusion: that had Sullivan seen the possibilities of humour in music he would have failed to illustrate the humour of Gilbert's words, for the reason that he would have demanded a far greater freedom than Gilbert's libretti gave him. Gilbert himself emphasized that the same humour always struck them in the same way: 'With Sullivan I never had to do that fatal thing—explain a joke'. The deep unconscious affinity implied here begot their perfect joint production of comedy, wit, and humour (in the technical meanings distinguished by psycho-analysis), with, in particular, the delicately-adjusted contribution of music to the pathos always inherent in 'humour'. Had Sullivan been a so-called witty musician, the affinity would have been missing; it was not merely that he 'triumphed in interpretation', but that he shared in the creation. Both men unconsciously knew humour for a serious matter, though each consciously longed for his oeuvre to be valued most on its literally serious side, in works produced, we may note, independently of each other.

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1: K. R. Eissler.  'The Effect of the Structure of the Ego on Psychoanalytic Technique.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1

K. R. Eissler. 'The Effect of the Structure of the Ego on Psychoanalytic Technique.'

Lack of distinction between three variables are responsible for the lowering of the standards of discussion of psycho-analytic technique. These three variables are: the patient's disorder and personality, the patient's present life circumstances and the psycho-analyst's personality. The paper deals with the first variable only.

The pitfalls of flexibility of technique, of face to face interviews, and of advising and encouraging the patient are examined. Transference is not a tool of therapy but a source of energy, the analyst's most effective tool is interpretation. Another tool is questioning the patient. There is, however, a principal difference between questioning in the course of the analytic process and for the purpose of conducting an interview. In the treatment of phobias the analyst may 'impose on the patient a command'. This is a tool of an entirely different type.

A deviation from the basic model technique of psycho-analysis might be called a parameter of the technique. Parameters have probably a lasting effect on the patient's transference which cannot be undone by intepretations. In neither schizophrenia nor delinquency can the technique of free association be applied; in both cases the whole technique must be changed in all essential aspects. Four criteria are laid down which a parameter has to fulfil if it can be accepted as being part of the psycho-analytic technique. Doubt is expressed whether schizophrenia has so far ever been treated by the basic model technique (free associations and interpretations). Psycho-analysis is effective in the treatment of schizophrenia, but there are fundamental differences when compared with the therapy of neurosis.

Parameters of psycho-analytic technique are enforced by those alterations in the patient's mental functioning and response which Freud called ego-modifications. A tentative comparison is made between neurotic and schizophrenic ego modifications. Among several parameters two are important in the treatment of schizophrenia—they are necessitated by the schizophrenic's specific ego-modification: goal construction and reduction of symptomatology. The concept of 'parameter' and the keeping within the four criteria for its analytic use will prevent new techniques from developing into 'wild psycho-analysis' and will enable us to study the underlying ego-modifications in schizophrenia as compared with those in the neuroses.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 3.: Joel Friedman and Sylvia Gassel.  'Orestes.  A Psycho-analytic Approach to Dramatic Criticism 11.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 3.

Joel Friedman and Sylvia Gassel. 'Orestes. A Psycho-analytic Approach to Dramatic Criticism 11.'

The authors have followed up their papers on Oedipus with a study of the legend of Orestes as described in the plays of Aeschylus, Sophocles and Euripides. The central point of the legend is that Orestes murders his mother in order to avenge his father, Agamemnon, who in his (Orestes') infancy had been slain by his mother, Clytemnestra, and her lover, Aegisthus, a blood-relative of Agamemnon. Orestes' crime is the reverse of Oedipus's and the myth is interpreted as depicting in the person of Orestes a psychology based on rejection of positive oedipal wishes by psychosexual inversion. Orestes reverses his father and rejects his mother, and in murdering her he not only avenges himself for her rejection of him—the invert's 'denial of loss of the breast'—but also denies the value of motherhood by hypercathecting the father (penis). The ambivalence of the society which created the myth is shown in Orestes' trial and acquittal. The rejection of the mother is not so complete as to render a trial unnecessary, but his crime is condoned as an upholding of the taboos of totem and incest, which have been broken by Clytemnestra and Aegisthus.

69

responseSet.title: Revue Française De Psychanalyse 15, 1951, No. 1.: Dr. Nacht.  'Presidential Address.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revue Française De Psychanalyse 15, 1951, No. 1.

Dr. Nacht. 'Presidential Address.'

The 13th Conference of French-speaking psycho-analysts will be devoted to the discussion of the relation of psycho-analysis to criminology. This has been decided on in anticipation of the 2nd International Congress of Criminologists, which will shortly meet in Paris. Psycho-analysis has made a very important contribution to modern criminology. The fact that infantile traumas play their part in the genesis of crime has thrown new light on the problem of criminal responsibility. Psycho-analysts are well aware of the practical difficulties with which modern criminology is faced, but they will insist on pointing out the contradictions in the present methods of administering the law. They will not be content with the simple alternative of asylum or prison. Psycho-analytic treatment in prisons is difficult, if not impossible, and some new way will have to be found in consequence of the contributions of psycho-analysis to criminology.

69

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revue Française De Psychanalyse 15, 1951, No. 1.

J. Lacan and M. Senac. 'The Theoretical Introduction of the Functions of Psycho-Analysis in Criminology.'

After defining their intention of ascertaining how psychology can help in the search for truth, the authors then consider 'the sociological realities of crime and law, and the relation of psycho-analysis to their dialectic foundation'. Neither crime nor the criminal are objects which can be considered apart from their sociological reference.

Every society from the primitive onwards has had laws, against which there are always transgressors, and every society manifests the relationship of crime to the law by punishment, which involves subjective assent.

69

responseSet.title: Revue Française De Psychanalyse 15, 1951, No. 1.: S. Lebovici, P. Male and F. Pasche.  'Clinical Report.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revue Française De Psychanalyse 15, 1951, No. 1.

S. Lebovici, P. Male and F. Pasche. 'Clinical Report.'

Claiming that psycho-analysis is a fruitful approach to criminology and drawing on their experience in this field the authors divide their study into three parts, considering in turn childhood, adolescence, and adult delinquency.

In the conflicting organization of the child it is possible to discern states of pre-delinquency which can effectively be treated by child-analysis, thus obviating anti-social difficulties at later ages.

In adolescence, maturation reawakens conflicts which are complicated by social changes, and at this period the incidence of delinquency rises. The authors have tried to appreciate the prognosis of juvenile delinquency apart from the question of responsibility.

In the case of adults, the authors do not look for therapeutic results with habitual criminals, but consider certain difficult cases and attempt to define therein the mechanisms leading to criminal acts, in which mechanisms of conflict play a determining role.

The child. The essential fact is the interrupted relationship between the child would-be murderer and the grown-up assassin, which raises the hope that we may come to understand the actual mechanism of criminal acts, for this can be observed in the child's disturbed behaviour and in the treatment situation. Commenting on the use of projective and other diagnostic tests, the authors state that they can contribute little to a vital understanding of the child, and they often place him in emotionally charged situations from which any conclusion with regard to his conflicts is doubtful.

It is very different with a psycho-analytic approach to the child which can reach feelings that underlie neurotic delinquency and are often of a self-punitive kind, as shown by clumsy and exhibitionistic anti-social acts; for example, the child who steals from the larder at home even though offered dainties by his mother. To punish such a child will only lead to further aggression and consequent guilt; thus a vicious circle will be caused, and further anti-social acts will be committed in order to seek punishment for relief.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, Journal of the Indian Psychoanalytical Society, 5, 1951, No. 1 and 2.

Edmund Bergler. 'The Patient's Suffering During Analysis—A Technical Mistake on the Part of the Analyst.'

Gordon Levinson

Gordon Levinson

Gordon Levinson  

Case material is given to support the author's contention that the 'suffering' patients endure in analysis, in connection with unsatisfied transference desires, debunking of rationalizations, and difficulty of free-association, is 'at bottom the patient's untouched psychic masochism'.

It is stated that all the phases of development which follow the basic oral stage 'are but desperate rescue and survival attempts to escape from the oral-masochistic danger'.

75

responseSet.title: Note on Aggressivity
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Note on Aggressivity

'Aggressivity.' A psychiatrist who broadcast recently in Woman's Hour used the term 'aggressivity.' A listener was moved to write this humourous protest:

It was in last Wednesday's session

that I heard a strange expression

from one of medical profession.

Maybe it's an indiscretion

to call it a vile transgression

which merits no concession

and should have quick suppression.

Maybe it's my obsession,

but I must make confession:

responseSet.title: Communication: The Social Matrix of Psychiatry
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Communication: The Social Matrix of Psychiatry

John Klauber

John Klauber

John Klauber  

By Jurgen Ruesch and Gregory Bateson. (New York: Norton, 1951. Pp. 289. Price $4.50.)

Conversation and Communication: A Psychological Inquiry. By Joost A. M. Meerloo. (New York: International Universities Press, 1952. Pp. 239. Price $4.00.)

In Communication: The Social Matrix of Psychiatry Professor Ruesch and Dr. Bateson seek to integrate current developmental trends in American psychiatry within a new theory of communication, and to criticize many assumptions of psychiatric epistemology in the light of their own epistemology based on their theory. The first half of the book, outlining the theory of communication, is written largely by Professor Ruesch, and the second more philosophical section largely by Dr. Bateson.

They regard the specialized conventions governing communication in any culture as specific for and determinant of that culture, and in formulating the manner in which communication works they utilize freely the theories of the cyberneticists. To communication itself they give the widest definition. Communication exists when A perceives that B perceives that A has sent a signal, and it operates at the intra-personal level of neural and humoral transmission as well as at the interpersonal group and cultural levels which depend chiefly on semantic transmission.

161

</artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: Personality and the Frontal Lobes
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Personality and the Frontal Lobes

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

By Asenath Petrie. (Routledge and Kegan Paul, London, 1952. Pp. 188. 21 s.)

Psychosurgical Problems. Ed. by Fred A. Mettler. (Routledge and Kegan Paul, London, 1952. Pp. 357. 42 s.)

The effects of leucotomy on symptoms and on the personality are of great theoretical interest to the psycho-analyst, distasteful though this treatment must be to him. Mrs. Petrie reports on psychological investigations carried out in twenty-seven neurotic patients before and after operation. They were cases of long-standing anxiety states and obsessional neurosis. The methods employed were those of "objective personality measurements' developed by Eysenck. Mrs. Petrie found that, as the result of the operation, the factor 'neuroticism' had decreased and that this change was more marked after the limited operation. As a reduction of that factor seems to go parallel with a decrease in neurotic symptoms, these methods may show a way of measuring the seriousness of the latter. However, it is doubtful whether they can be of help in other than surgical treatments. 'Neuroticism' is supposed to measure an innate predisposition which can be expected to be altered by an operation on the brain but hardly by psychological treatment. In her investigations into the intellectual aspects of personality Mrs. Petrie made some interesting observations. She found that after extensive operation a change in the use of language took place; the patients appeared to neglect the standards they had previously set themselves with regard to exactitude in verbal expression. She suggests that this change in the use of language can be described in terms of super-ego function, and she regards the frontal lobes as the areas of the brain concerned with that aspect of the personality. The super-ego is, of course, not the only part of the personality that is affected by that treatment; ego and id functions suffer as well,, and the super-ego changes can be understood only if changes in the other areas of the personality are also considered. This was outside the scope of Mrs. Petrie's investigations. There is much that remains problematical about the personality changes following operation on the frontal lobes, but this book is valuable because it has narrowed the area of ignorance. It is very lucidly written and extremely readable.

responseSet.title: Shakespeare's Sonneten. En Hun Verband Met De Travesti-Double Spelen: By Dr. C. Van Emde Boas.  (Amsterdam, Antwerp:  Wereld Bibliotheek, 1951.  Pp. 528.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Shakespeare's Sonneten. En Hun Verband Met De Travesti-Double Spelen

By Dr. C. Van Emde Boas. (Amsterdam, Antwerp: Wereld Bibliotheek, 1951. Pp. 528.)

C. Gomperts

C. Gomperts

C. Gomperts  

This is a very detailed psycho-analytical study of Shakespeare's Sonnets and several of his plays, with the aid of which the author attempts to solve the much discussed mystery behind the triangular situation created by the Poet, the Dark Lady, and the Fair Youth of the Sonnets. The literature concerning itself directly or indirectly with this theme is certainly most profuse, and the various interpretations, explanations, and apologies by many commentators have given rise throughout the centuries to a great deal of heated controversy and to irreconcilable achools of thought.

In his introduction the author writes: 'The structure of this study reflects the chronological development of the conclusions reached in the way in which this would be carried out in therapeutic treatment. Furthermore, it fully bears the character of a case study with all the unexpected turns pertaining to it.'

A psycho-analytic study of this kind, based on very meagre historical data on the one hand and on creations of a high artistic level on the other, has of necessity its self-imposed limitations with regard to that degree of scientific accuracy which, by comparison, we would aim at in a clinical case study. However convincing the conclusions may seem (and Dr. Van Emde Boas reaches many conclusions which sound very convincing) the most important corroborative material, i.e. Shakespeare's own free associations, are lacking. It must be stated in fairness that the author is aware of this, and he does not make more than modest claims for the accuracy of his findings.

Two main themes constitute the foci of this extensive study: the poet's depression which is generally believed to have set in round 1601, and his latent homosexuality (subject homo-eroticism) which, in the author's view plays the most important part in the genesis and development of his depressed condition. A substantial part of the book is taken up in tracing these latent homosexual character traits and the 'friendship' sonnets to the 'Fair Youth' are extensively analysed for this purpose. In the sonnets which refer to the triangular drama the poet loses his beloved Fair Youth to the Dark Lady. At the same time each of the participants in the triangular setting stands in some kind of love relationship to the other two, though ultimately the poet loses Dark Lady and Fair Youth to one another.

responseSet.title: The Will to Live: By Arnold Hutschnecker, M.D.  (London:  Victor Gollancz, 1952.  Pp. 278.  13 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Will to Live

By Arnold Hutschnecker, M.D. (London: Victor Gollancz, 1952. Pp. 278. 13 s. 6 d.)

James Taylor

James Taylor

James Taylor  

Sometime in the past century, following the discovery of electrical currents in living tissue, somebody invented positive and negative metal rods which were used by the physician to stroke any diseased part of the body and in theory to re-direct the supposedly disordered electrical currents. Many people felt the better for their use, and sermons were even preached urging the government to provide a pair of rods for each household. Then a doubting doctor had a pair of wooden rods made to look like metal, and found his results were as good as with the metal ones. General revulsion of feeling was so strong that six months later the rods had been forgotten. Dramatically similar was the immediate abandonment by the public of Yadil, following its exposure in the early twenties.

Dr. Hutschnecker has his 'rods'—handy antitheses such as Life Instinct and Death Instinct, Aggressive and Regressive Diseases and (blessed word) Psycho-Somatic! He makes great play with them to the accompaniment of glib verbigerative chatter and reminiscence.

As is necessary in all such systems of thought, one pole can become the opposite pole at a moment's notice without the least harm to anybody. In the same paragraph he speaks of ACTH as perhaps inducing an infectious disease and therefore dangerous to use where such a condition is suspected; and then goes on to give an example of its use in an infectious disease where it had marvellously curative effect.

It is a pity that the publisher in his explanatory note on the dust-jacket should use, even in the most remote way, the word 'psycho-analytical' in connection with this book.

166

responseSet.title: Infant Speech: By M. M. Lewis, M.A., Ph.D.  (London:  Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd., 1951.  Pp. 383.  28 s. net.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infant Speech

By M. M. Lewis, M.A., Ph.D. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd., 1951. Pp. 383. 28 s. net.)

Sadie Mervis

Sadie Mervis

Sadie Mervis  

In his preface to the 1st edition of this book, published in 1936, Dr. Lewis says 'one must have faith in the continuity of growth'. He has done more in this book than ask us to have faith; he has provided evidence. Slightly less than one-third of the book is devoted to the appendices, containing tabulated data based on the observations of a number of workers, including the author's own observations of a boy K. Some of the appendices and two further chapters have been added to this new edition of the book. Dr. Lewis has organized his own observations and conclusions together with those of such scientific observers as Darwin, Bühler, Preyer, Deville, Ament, Guillame, Hoyer, Valentine, and Stern.

This book is devoted to discussion, fully documented from observations, of such phases as early utterance, early response to speech, babbling, imitation, the beginning of comprehension of conventional speech, the beginning of meaningful use of conventional speech, mastery of conventional forms, and the beginning and early functions of questions. While each chapter makes its conscientious offerings of observations and explanations, the illustrative material in this review is drawn mainly from the first half of the book, relating to the earliest phases in speech development. Dr. Lewis has illustrated richly the fluid and continuous development of speech from the earliest infant cries to the fully-fledged adult word conversation. The student of human behaviour who is tempted to wander into the 'controversial area' will perhaps be inclined to abandon the term 'preverbal period', after studying and speculating about the observations recorded in this book.

The author sets out to solve two problems. (1) The manner in which speech begins in children, and (2) How those characteristics of speech which we usually see flowering during the course of the child's second year are related to his earlier experience and behaviour. This detailed and valuable study will not disappoint readers who are aware from the outset that Dr. Lewis apparently does not include a postulate of the unconscious, its mechanisms and fantasies, in his explanations of 'experience and behaviour'. In an eclectic bibliography of 119 references, both psychological and linguistic, psycho-analysis is represented by Freud's Beyond the Pleasure Principle.

responseSet.title: The World Next Door: By Fritz Peters.  (London:  Gollancz, 1949.  Pp. 320.  12 s. 6 d.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The World Next Door

By Fritz Peters. (London: Gollancz, 1949. Pp. 320. 12 s. 6 d.)

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle  

The writer, a young American, gives a graphic account of his experiences in a mental hospital during his three months' stay following a schizophrenic episode. From the dates given the book must have been written within about a year from the time of his discharge, and the contents must therefore be regarded primarily as a reminiscence, though the style adopted is that of the narrative.

The coherent manner in which he has pieced together the elements of his confused thinking and made them into an intelligible whole would indicate some considerable degree of consolidation and possibly refute the diagnosis of schizophrenia.

The narrative begins with an outline of the thoughts which haunted him the night before the onset of the acute phase, which necessitated restraint. The journey to the hospital is worthy of note, as he was taken there in a police car, handcuffed and escorted by two policemen. The story takes us in considerable detail, through the admission block, pack room, acute ward to an early release on licence. Here he is seen in the family where there is no empathy. A misunderstanding with his mother provokes an acute outburst of aggression and he is returned to hospital, again by the police and handcuffed. He was returned to his old ward, and after a short period given E.C.T., and from that graduated through O.T. to his final discharge.

The narrative can be taken in two ways:

1. As a straightforward clinical record its value is impaired by the lack of previous history, hospital records, and notes on subsequent adjustment. However, it does clearly demonstrate how a nursing staff trained in psycho-analytic principles could help the deluded patient, for his main grievance was that all his questions were evaded or answered foolishly. After his readmission he felt that there was a change in attitude towards him, and he shows that through the development of a sympathetic, human relationship with some patients and nurses and an understanding relationship with some of the doctors he was able to come to terms with his deep-seated aggression.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Man Into Wolf: An Anthropological Interpretation of Sadism, Masochism and Lycanthropy

By Robert Eisler. With an Introduction by Prof. Sir David K. Henderson. (London: Routledge and Kegan Paul Ltd., 1951. Pp. 286. 21 s.)

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle

D. Hardcastle  

The form of this book is somewhat unusual. It consists essentially of a 29-page lecture given to the psychiatric section of the Royal Society of Medicine under the presidency of Sir D. K. Henderson (who writes the Introduction), the author's foreword, and two hundred pages of notes, five appendices, and an index. The subject-matter was originally published in the Hibbert Journal for 1946 as one of the initial chapters of a Sociology of war and peace, but the author's untimely death in 1949 prevented him from completing this work.

The author's foreword is a valuable contribution, as in it he outlines the theme of his paper and conclusively defends its somewhat unorthodox presentation in the book. He declares that in presenting a thesis the lecturer should be able to answer all questions and counter all objections raised in the subsequent discussion. This he claims to have done by including in the book so many notes and references. It is obvious from Sir D. K. Henderson's introduction that the lecture was clear and interesting, but perhaps for the reader the subject-matter would have been more coherently presented if the lecture had been recast and more of the supporting evidence now given in the form of notes incorporated in the text. It is clear that the author spent an immense amount of time and thought on both the lecture itself and its supporting notes.

In his approach to the understanding of sadism and masochism he introduces the paradox of the widespread desire to suffer pain, and uses this to show how ridiculous is the hedonistic theory of motivation. He ignores any ontogenetic causes and explains irrational behaviour in terms of atavistic reversion.

170

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Sex. Its Meaning and Purpose

By W. E. Sargent. The Teach Yourself Books, edited by Leonard Cutts. (London: English Universities Press Ltd., 1951. Pp. 154. 6 s.)

S. Yates

S. Yates

S. Yates  

Books on sex tend too often to be religious, sensational, or coldly scientific; the author of this book is to be congratulated in that he falls into none of these categories. He has tried to establish real values and to point the way to a new approach to sexual morality. It is a book crammed full of facts suitable for any intelligent reader. Over-simplification detracts from its value to some extent, and this is specially the case when he discusses sublimation (page 41). It is very questionable whether masturbation should be included in the section 'The Deviations from the Normal'.

171

responseSet.title: Psychiatrie Heute (Psychiatry To-Day)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychiatrie Heute (Psychiatry To-Day)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

By Kurt Schneider. (Stuttgart: George Thieme, 1952. Pp. 31.)

Die Grenzen der Psychotherapie (The Limitations of Psychotherapy). By G. Ewald. (Stuttgart, 1952. Pp. 35.)

Gesetze und Sinn des Träumens (Laws and Meaning of Dreaming). By K. Leonhard. (Stuttgart, 1951. Pp. 146.)

171

German psychiatry has for some time been stagnant. In the fields in which it excelled, progress has come to a standstill: descriptive psychiatry, as Professor Schneider frankly admits, has come to the end of its resources; the same can be said of neuro-pathology; and psychiatric eugenics stands discredited. The attitude of most German psychiatrists to the dynamic approach based on psycho-analysis is one of rejection or bewilderment. Having fought psycho-analysis tooth and nail, they now find that, while it is prohibited in the East for ideological reasons, it has conquered most of the important teaching centres in the United States. The reactions to this situation are interesting to watch. The sense of frustration in the proud centres of German psychiatric teaching and research must be considerable. Frustration is apt to lead to regression. And this is exactly what threatens to happen to German psychiatry, which shows distinct signs of again embracing philosophy, from which it had emancipated itself only with the greatest difficulties. In many University Clinics the old captains are still at the helm. The younger generation has not arrived. It is against this historical background that the first two of these publications have to be appraised. The third is typical of quite a number of publications in Germany and elsewhere. It deals with a subject on which psycho-analysis has thrown much light. Having recognized this in generous terms the authors ignore the psycho-analytic contribution more or less completely, and produce a 'purified' version of their own.

Professor Schneider of Heidelberg reflects on the present situation of psychiatry: it rests on two pillars, neuropathology and psychopathology, the latter being, of course, of a phenomenological type. The unity of body and mind, which psychiatry takes for granted, is an irrational and metaphysical fact. Here the author follows N. Hartmann, who regards all problems which have an irreducible irrational element as metaphysical. Thus psychiatry becomes metaphysics. Mental abnormalities may be variations from the average, such as the psychopathies which include the neuroses. They are mainly constitutionally determined. Only the organic dementias are illnesses in the medical sense. Whether the major psychoses are illnesses in that sense is still uncertain, though most psychiatric work is based on that hypothesis. The alternative is existentialist psychology, which regards the major psychoses as primary aberrations of the mind. Schneider is obviously attracted by this approach, although he sees clearly that it implies a break with the monistic tenet of body-mind unity and leads away from biology. He also realizes that such an approach is bound to introduce Traducianism, the belief in the transmission of the soul, as the only explanation for heredity in mental disorders. Professor Schneider, who has been strongly influenced by the philosopher Jaspers, deserves thanks for stating the psychiatrist's dilemma so clearly.

responseSet.title: Mystique Et Continence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mystique Et Continence

C. Gomperts

C. Gomperts

C. Gomperts  

Les Etudes Carmelitaines. Desclée de Brouwer, 1952. (Pp. 407.)

This volume consists of papers presented at the 7th International Catholic Congress of Religious Psychology held at Avon. The task it set itself was to confront the findings obtained through mystical experience with those brought to light by scientific workers principally in the fields of psychology and psychiatry. The main question to which an answer is searched for in these papers is—to put it in a nutshell—whether sexuality and a mystical-spiritual way of life, as demanded by the Catholic Church from its priests, are mutually antagonistic or in some way compatible with each other. The contributions have come, apart from the Church (Catholic and others), also from other distinguished authors in the fields of religious philosophy, physiology, psychology, psychiatry, and psycho-analysis. While the profuse variety of these papers—there are twenty-six—offers much that is of interest and also many opinions with which one could personally agree or disagree, there is, too, another noteworthy feature in that several of the Church representatives show a basic acquaintance with the knowledge of the human mind gained during the last half century.

The query which seems to occupy the thoughts of many of the writers—implicitly or explicitly—could be put thus: Why do certain people tolerate sexual abstinence better than others; why do some remain healthy, vigorous, and capable of a satisfactory object relationship, whereas others are not equal to the task and fall ill as a result?

Our patients teach us that there is no single answer of general validity to this complex question, and that factors such as inherited constitution (organic and psychic), early, pre-disposing external influences—and, of course, many more—have to be taken into account. Furthermore, we know from clinical experience that to many people the opposite applies: they can remain comparatively healthy only as long as they can succeed in avoiding certain instinctual demands or in keeping these at a constant low charge.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: Adelaide McF.  Johnson.  'Some Etiological Aspects of Repression, Guilt and Hostility.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

Adelaide McF. Johnson. 'Some Etiological Aspects of Repression, Guilt and Hostility.'

This paper attempts to re-evaluate the concepts of repression, restitution and hostility in the light of observations made by anthropologists and collaborative therapists working with parent and child on the relative importance of biological and environmental factors in early development.

In contradistinction to the 'classical' view that repression is a normal mechanism provoked by an innate antagonism between ego and id or a discrepancy between their rates of development (instinctual anxiety) it is suggested that it is a mechanism of adaptation to pathological environmental conditions and that 'the extent to which repression occurs is one index of the degree of departure from good health'.

The development of guilt and restitutive drives in children with cold, unloving parents is only explicable on the assumption that it is a realistic attempt to appease the source of gratification of minimal, primary needs. 'Our concept of the meaning of guilt should be expanded to the more inclusive view that guilt is a need for punishment in an urge to make reparation to a "needed object" rather than limiting the meaning to the common situation in which restitutive behaviour arises from parental love and the child's fear of losing it.'

'Hostile aggressiveness, as distinguished from biologically inherited aggressiveness, originates in the course of the infant's incorporation of the total maternal attitude, which includes the mother's own hostility.

271

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: S. S. Feldman.  'Anxiety and Orgasm.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

S. S. Feldman. 'Anxiety and Orgasm.'

This paper is concerned with the problems raised by the occurrence in both waking and dream life of anxiety accompanied by genital excitement. This combination, which can be seen in anxiety and punishment dreams, beating and other sadomasochistic phantasies, examination dreams, stage fright, etc., is traced by the author to a repressed, incestuous, sexual drive which has been activated by sexual frustration or by some stimulus, which by association has become connected with repressed sexuality. The ego in such circumstances may use anxiety as a partial defence and act as if it wished and accepted punishment, even though it is more or less aware of the underlying sexual impulse. The tension between ego and super-ego is perceived as both painful and pleasurable, libido and anxiety co-existing. 'It is, in other words, a hypocritical as if state in which the ego perceives anxiety on one level and lust on another.

271

Merrill.  'Childhood Attitudes toward Flatulence and their Possible Relation to Adult Character.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: Bruce R. Merrill.  'Childhood Attitudes toward Flatulence and their Possible Relation to Adult Character.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

Bruce R. Merrill. 'Childhood Attitudes toward Flatulence and their Possible Relation to Adult Character.'

The author describes a small group of men to whom flatulence was of especial psychological significance. Though they all showed obsessive features, their characters deviated markedly from the 'anal type' described by Freud. They were arrogant, boastful and fraudulent, possessed a ribald, sarcastic wit and a tendency to use unusually foul language, and used passing flatus for aggressive and exhibitionist purposes. They showed a similarity of childhood developmental patterns. At the phallic level their flatulence represented an inadequate identification with the father. At the anal level, they showed an inadequate enjoyment of, and capacity for, achievement. A disturbed oral development was evidenced by impulsiveness and inadequate super-ego development.

271

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: George Gerö.  'The Concept of Defense.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

George Gerö. 'The Concept of Defense.'

This discussion of the concept of defence is summarized by the author: 'I have attempted to indicate some of the problems involved in the conception of defence. In a restricted sense, defence is viewed as the sum of those unconscious ego activities which work towards maintaining a neurotic equilibrium and thus hinder the emergence of the transference neurosis; they tend to prevent the crystallization of the neurotic conflicts in the analytic process. Defences are directed against instinctual drives as well as against anxiety, and those vanguards of the drives, the emotions.

271

Niederland.  'Three Notes on the Schreber Case.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: William G. Niederland.  'Three Notes on the Schreber Case.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

William G. Niederland. 'Three Notes on the Schreber Case.'

These three glosses on Freud's classic study deal with:

1. The onset of Schreber two illnesses. Freud only concerned himself with the later of Schreber's two breakdowns, the onset of which he attributed, in part at least, to the somatic factor of the male climacteric. Niederland points out that this factor cannot be implicated in Schreber's first illness eight or nine years earlier and draws attention to the fact that the first illness began when Schreber was a candidate for the Reichstag, while the second followed his appointment as Senatpraesident of Saxony. In each case reality demanded of him an active masculine role, and the author suggests that this demand was the precipitating factor, which, in both illnesses, released his latent passive feminine tendencies.

2. Linguistic observations. The author shows how various apparently meaningless passages of Schreber's Memoirs become intelligible if the full German, and particularly South German, range of meanings of words in the original text is taken into account.

3. The 'ascent from Flechsig to God' in Schreber's delusional system. Schreber's progressive deification of his physician, and his unconscious equation of him with his father, is traced through the series of delusional names Schreber gave him.

272

responseSet.documentRef: (1953) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: Gustav Bychowski.  'Metapsychology of Artistic Creation.'. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  34:272

responseSet.documentInfoXML: <artinfo arttype="ABS" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ABS" id="IJP.034.0272B" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1953</artyear><artvol actual="34">34</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">272</artpgrg><artvar>B</artvar><arttitle> The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4</arttitle><artsub>Gustav Bychowski.  'Metapsychology of Artistic Creation.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4: Gustav Bychowski.  'Metapsychology of Artistic Creation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 20, 1951, No. 4

Gustav Bychowski. 'Metapsychology of Artistic Creation.'

Although the defensive character of the creative impulse is often clinically detectable, it is probable that it more often arises from less pathological sources, among which narcissistic omnipotence is preponderant. The artist wishes to create imperishable objects and to perpetuate the past in defiance of the inevitability of change and oblivion. His desire to perpetuate the past includes the desire to relieve the painful; in this respect the artist's ego resembles that of the ego in traumatic neurosis. He tends to relive his childhood by projecting both his repressed id impulses and unconscious super-ego components on to objects of his own creation. His stock of projectible objects is greatly increased by his ability to split off parts of his ego, which are maintained in incomplete repression, ready to re-emerge when called upon by the creative ego. This faculty of the creative ego, when compared with the ego of the pre-psychotic and of 'ordinary' people, presupposes (a) an unusual strength of ego and capacity for binding energy released from the unconscious, and (b) an unusual mobility of counter-cathexis and psychic barriers (including the ego-boundaries), which is responsible for the creative ego's easy access to the unconscious and for its ability to perceive external reality in terms of symbolic meaning and to express intra-psychic experiences in terms of material reality.

During the creative process libido becomes deflected in three directions; (1) towards the objects created by the artist, which absorb a major part of his narcissistic and object libido; (2) towards parts of reality which become invested with narcissistic libido; and (3) libido becomes diverted from its original instinctual aims to provide the energy for creation. This last is the process Freud called sublimation. Sublimation in the artist differs from that in other civilized persons in its scope and intensity. It may well be that in certain instances there is a re-sexualization of sublimated impulses leading to an unusual intensity and vividness in the resulting work of art.

responseSet.documentRef: (1953) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1: Gregory Zilboorg.  'The Emotional Problem and the Therapeutic Role of Insight.'. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  34:272-273
responseSet.documentRefHTML: (1953) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1: Gregory Zilboorg. 'The Emotional Problem and the Therapeutic Role of Insight.'. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 34:272-273

responseSet.documentInfoXML: <artinfo arttype="ABS" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ABS" id="IJP.034.0272F" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1953</artyear><artvol actual="34">34</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">272-273</artpgrg><artvar>F</artvar><arttitle> The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1</arttitle><artsub>Gregory Zilboorg.  'The Emotional Problem and the Therapeutic Role of Insight.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1: Gregory Zilboorg.  'The Emotional Problem and the Therapeutic Role of Insight.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1

Gregory Zilboorg. 'The Emotional Problem and the Therapeutic Role of Insight.'

The lack of clarity that attaches to the concept of insight is due to the fact that the term has two opposed meanings, a psychiatric and a psycho-analytical one. To the psychiatrist insight is something positive which can be acquired and consists

272

of an intellectual awareness of motives, etc., accompanied by 'appropriate' affect and 'emotional understanding'. What constitutes an appropriate accompanying affect for intellectual insight and what the 'emotional component' of intellectual understanding is, is never defined. This view of insight is responsible for the popular but erroneous beliefs that a patient will lose his symptoms when he discovers what caused them and that insight is something that a therapist can give his patient. The psycho-analytical view of insight is that it is a state of ego functioning which arises naturally when the patient is freed of the surplus of unconscious affects which prevents him feeling what he feels and seeing what he sees. It appears as a byproduct of psychological liberation and 'should be considered as the ultimate and crowning point of integration of ego functioning'. From this point of view there can be no question of 'giving' insight.

What is the nature of the insight that is necessary for the preservation of good therapeutic results? It is obviously not solely a question of the strength of the ego, since there are individuals with strong ego organization whose neurotic character is undeniable. It is indeed, Zilboorg suggests, personalities of this kind with a 'smugly integrated ego' who in our professional and academic circles are 'largely responsible for such purely intellectual concepts as emotional or unconscious insight' and 'for the neat yet loosely knit interpretations of cultural, racial, or national patterns'. Psychotherapies based on this kind of intellectualism, in which the therapist actively dispenses insight, constitute a return of repressed resistances to depth psychology, however much they may masquerade as innovations based on ego psychology. The insight these therapies give can only produce results by strengthening the repressive forces of the ego. Lasting non-defensive insight is, on the contrary, based on a real re-integration of the ego which can be only acquired through a gradual process of re-living and working through in the transference. 'Such insight is a purely affective process in the wake of which follows rational and affective appreciation of a new orientation of the ego toward the world and toward one's own self.

responseSet.documentRef: (1953) The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1: Thomas S. Szasz.  'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Instincts.'. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  34:273

Szasz.  'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Instincts.'</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1: Thomas S. Szasz.  'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Instincts.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1

Thomas S. Szasz. 'On the Psychoanalytic Theory of Instincts.'

This paper examines some of the basic concepts underlying Freud's theory of the Life and Death instincts. It is pointed out that Freud based his hypothesis of a Death instinct on the assumption that Fechner's principle, a special case of the second law of thermodynamics (the principle of increasing entropy), was applicable to living organisms. In fact it is valid only for closed systems, whereas living matter is an open system characterized and, indeed, definable by its ability to take energy (negative entropy) from its environment. The hypothesis is therefore suggested that there is but one primary instinct, a Life instinct, the aim of which is to maintain the life processes of any given system in continued operation.

Carrel's tissue culture experiments are cited as consistent with this hypothesis. Under optimal metabolic conditions, such as probably no longer ever occur in nature, issues grow indefinitely and are potentially immortal, ageing and death apparently only occurring as a response to environmental interference with the life instinct. Carrel's experiments show further that when the life instinct operates unopposed there is only growth and no development of new structures or functions. Adaptation is therefore the result of frustration. Two kinds of adaptation alone are possible, progressive with increasing differentiation and regressive with loss of differentiation. Among the factors affecting the choice of mode of adaptation the degree of complexity of the organism is of paramount importance, primitive systems tending to adapt progressively, complex ones regressively.

A full bibliography of the literature on the Death instinct is appended.

273

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Quarterly, 21, 1952, No. 1

Richard Renneker. 'Dream Timing.'

Analysis of the dreams of a patient who possessed the ability to awaken at a precise predetermined time led to the conclusion that he timed his dreams so that the security of knowing that his dreams would be interrupted by his alarm clock was used as a defence against the emergence of anxiety.

273

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1

Robert P. Knight. The Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association.

On behalf of the American Psychoanalytic Association its President announces 'the birth of a new psychoanalytic journal'. Its cordial reception by, and relationship with, the three older psycho-analytic journals is stressed. The new journal will be the 'official' journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association by virtue of the inclusion of the Bulletin of the American Psychoanalytic Association. The new journal should 'prove to have met an essential need of the members of the Association and of other professional people, here and abroad, who follow the progress of psycho-analytic thinking'.

274

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1: George Gero.  'Defences in Symptom Formation.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1

George Gero. 'Defences in Symptom Formation.'

Studying phobic symptomatology, the author shows that displacement in phobias is a biphasic act. Symptom formation begins with an inhibitory act, i.e. repression and fantasy formation, and is followed by an unconscious ego activity which materializes in phobic avoidance. It is more difficult to describe the defensive activities in obsessive thoughts which present themselves as symptoms. The content of the obsessive thought is easy to interpret. (In the case quoted it was oedipal and preoedipal hostility.) The obsessive thought appears to be a release of the original now desexualized impulse. The desexualization creates the character of emptiness and alienation which marks the obsessive experience. The affectlessness of the obsessive patient gives way to anxiety whenever in the analytic process the defences are understood and analysed.

275

responseSet.title: Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1: Mark Kanzer.  'Past and Present in the Transference.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Journal of the American Psychoanalytic Association, 1, 1953, No. 1

Mark Kanzer. 'Past and Present in the Transference.'

W. H.

W. H.

W. H.  

A succinct statement of the author's basic assumptions says: 'Whereas the pressure of the past impels the ego to behave as if it were the id, according to the formula put forward by Nunberg, it is equally true that the pressure of the present may demand of the id that it behaves as if it were the ego—i.e. so that regressive mechanisms may be employed to control reality.' The reconstruction of the past goes along with that of the present. Analysis of the transference both in its progressive and regressive aspect are the prerequisites of genetic reconstruction. The analysis of the past allows anticipation and modification of behaviour in the present; this becomes apparent in the transference neurosis.

276

responseSet.documentInfoXML: <artinfo arttype="ABS" artsource="A1V2" j="IJP" artsubtype="ABS" id="IJP.034.0276C" ISSN="0020-7578"><artyear>1953</artyear><artvol actual="34">34</artvol><artpgrg style="arabic">276-277</artpgrg><artvar>C</artvar><arttitle> The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952</arttitle><artsub>Heinz Hartmann:  'The Mutual Influences in the Development of Ego and Id', pp. 9–30.</artsub><artauth hidden="false"><none/></artauth></artinfo>
responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952: Heinz Hartmann:  'The Mutual Influences in the Development of Ego and Id', pp. 9–30.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Heinz Hartmann: 'The Mutual Influences in the Development of Ego and Id', pp. 9–30.

In the course of Freud's writings the concept 'ego' underwent numerous changes. Psycho-analysts are not always aware of the numerous drawbacks of using the word 'ego' and attaching to it not a general but a specific meaning; hence they speak of the reality ego, the defensive ego, the organizing, the rational, the social ego. Since psycho-analysis aims at a final integration with general psychology the concept ego should be taken as a general one, and when the term is used as a 'partial concept', such use should not contradict the general meaning.

The interdependence of id and ego can be shown from the influences which the ego exerts on the id. The ego imposes on the id changes of aims and of the modes of energy. Desexualization and a similar process in respect of the aggressive drive are examples of this. On the other hand instincts lend some patterns for the development of the ego's defence mechanisms. Individual variations of ego development can be observed which have a decisive influence on the instincts, e.g. on the timing of the emergence of the partial drives and the forms in which these partial drives manifest themselves (instinct development). Sleep and psychoses are well suited for the study of early ego development. The id-ego relationship can further be elicited through the study of the development of the body ego and of object-relationships. The most profound knowledge of the id-ego relationship has come from the understanding of psychic conflicts. Closer exploration has led the author to formulate his findings in terms of the 'non-conflictual sphere of the ego'. This sphere is a potent determinant of the methods by which instinctual stimuli are dealt with.

At the onset of extrauterine life there exists neither an id nor an ego in the sense in which these terms have been used in psycho-analysis; an undifferentiated phase has to be assumed to operate at first. This view agrees with Freud's own formulations.

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952: S. Nacht, M.D., pp. 54–59.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

S. Nacht, M.D., pp. 54–59.

The author rejects Hartmann's proposition of an 'autonomous sphere of the ego', which he finds to remain unsubstantiated. In his opinion Loewenstein's illustrations of such a sphere can as well be interpreted according to Freud's early view on the development of the ego as sequelae of the interaction of id-drives with the environment.

In the new-born certain automatisms have been observed which disappear immediately after birth but reappear again in the course of progressing ego-development. The energic source of all ego functions has to be looked for in the id-drives. The author agrees with Hartmann and Hoffer that the physiological approach cannot be dispensed with. Modern physiological studies have reinforced the argument that the link between neurovegetative, endocrine, and other functions and mental functioning is a very close one. Attention is drawn to Selye's work. The interdependence of both systems can be substantiated by direct child observation as it has been carried out by Anna Freud and Spitz.

Most important in the study of the id-ego relationship is the degree of integration of aggression and the problems arising from it. Since fear acting as 'aggressor' (Selye) may produce excitation of the hypophysis and hypothalamus, it thus acts on the drives by determining an increase or decrease in a quantitative sense. Fear (the response to insecurity) 'represents perhaps the most tangible instance of transition from the psychic to the somatic level that we are able to discern'. The stabilization of ego functions modifies the person's total, physiological make-up.

278

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952: W. Clifford M. Scott, M.D., pp. 60–65.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

W. Clifford M. Scott, M.D., pp. 60–65.

Semantic difficulties will be overcome by repeated discussion of the field under examination. Consciousness, the road to the unconscious, has been rather neglected so far. It is everywhere accepted that hypotheses can be discarded as soon as they are falsified. Melanie Klein attempted to falsify the concept of primary narcissism. In the author's opinion primary narcissism is susceptible of analysis in terms of object relationships. Such object relations seem to be between an early ego which are neither stably internal nor external. 'With fragmentation stability of internalization and externalization disappears. Fragmentation appears to be dynamic and not regressive to a preintegrated state of ego nuclei such as Glover proposed.'

The testing of the death instinct theory has in the past led us to the study of sleep and infancy; in future it will lead us to the study of old age and dying.

One of the earliest mechanisms of inhibition is sleep, actual or hallucinated. Sleep can be aggressive. How far is this allied to the silent infantile helplessness?

278

responseSet.title: The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952: Else Pappenheim, M.D., and M. Swenney.  'Separation Anxiety in Mother and Child', pp. 95–114.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Else Pappenheim, M.D., and M. Swenney. 'Separation Anxiety in Mother and Child', pp. 95–114.

An account is given of a case worker's success in enabling a four-year-old child to separate from his mother for attendance at nursery school. The dynamic factors in the complex relationship of mother and child are discussed on the basis of observation made in twenty-eight interviews. The child's feeling of rejection was understood and handled mainly in terms of his awareness of his mother's motives, while the traumatic factor in his acute anxiety—an earlier hospitalization with phantasies of being abandoned—became known only after treatment had ceased. An attempt is made to estimate the relative decisiveness in the success of the actual interpretative work, the new educational approach offered to the mother, and the modifying influence of the worker's warm personality on the relationship. An interesting reaction by the child to misinterpretation is discussed.

279

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Melitta Sperling, M.D. 'Animal Phobias in a Two-Year Child', pp. 115–125.

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas

Ruth Thomas  

This case is of particular interest for the study of the sources of anxiety and of symptom formation in a very young child who was taken into analysis at twenty-three months. The severe progressive symptomatology from seven months onwards following sudden weaning and enforced cleanliness training in a somewhat rejective maternal relationship

279

gives a clear picture of an acute primary disturbance. Temporary breaks with reality were precipitated by the inability to deal with sadistic pregenital elements, mainly oral. The birth of a brother at twenty-six months added night terrors and animal phobias to an already complex clinical picture. The function of the phobias in limiting the loss of reality and maintaining essential functions and relationships appeared clearly in the analysis. Similarly in the nightmares, the less guarded sadistic manifestations against the mother forced the child into waking to seek real maternal comfort. Both symptoms ensured a precarious hold on reality and prevented a lapse into paranoid or depressive psychosis.

280

[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Marie Bonaparte. 'Masturbation and Death or a Compulsive Confession of Masturbation', pp. 170–172.

Subsequent to a threat by her nurse that she would die if she continued to masturbate, a girl of nine developed an obsessive urge to ask questions, mainly about 'buttons'. The analytic exploration showed the role of a primal scene experience, of the penis envy, of the death of the girl's mother in childbirth and the significance of the obsessive questioning as a form of expiation for herself as well as for the nurse.

280

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Selma Fraiberg. 'A Critical Neurosis in a Two-and-a-Half-Year-Old Girl', pp. 173–215.

This is a full account of the psycho-analytic treatment of a severely disturbed girl of such early age, on which the research of many analysts has been centred for some time. The case history and the author's conclusions as to the dynamics and structure of the case are well-integrated accounts of relevant facts, reactions, and of the reactions in the treatment situation.

280

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

David Beres, M.D. 'Clinical Notes on Aggression in Children', pp. 241–263.

This paper offers a number of clinical studies of aggressive manifestations in children in latency and adolescence, and discusses the diagnostic considerations which lead to the differentiation of two dynamically separate groups of aggression. In the first group, the evidence of the immature ego's inability to deal with aggressive irruptions from the id is discussed in relation to considerations of general ego weakness, the state of object relations and of primitive defences. The author makes striking use of recent contributions to ego psychology, notably by Anna Freud, Beata Rank, and Hartmann, Kris, and Lowenstein. The second group covers disturbances at different levels of libidinal development resulting in unsatisfactory fusion with aggressive drives (sado-masochism). The paper is concerned to demonstrate the importance of the conception of aggression as an instinctual drive for the elucidation of these problems, and opposes the contention of Franz Alexander that aggression is mainly an adeptive phenomenon.

280

W. H.

W. H.

W. H.  

Thirty-seven cases of learning difficulties are surveyed, the results confronted with the relevant literature, and the diminished capacity to learn related to the various underlying causes and conflicts, such as organic defects and illness, anxieties, object relations, inhibitions, neurotic illness and ego maturation.

281

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psychoanalytic Study of the Child (An Annual) 7, 1952

Eric Lindemann, Ph.D., M.D., and Lydia G. Dawes, M.D. 'The Use of Psycho-analytic Constructs in Preventive Psychiatry', pp. 429–448.

W. H.

W. H.

W. H.  

Concurring with Kubie, the authors believe 'that the psycho-analyst is the logical person to initiate efforts in preventive psychiatry'; but they stress the impediment caused by the small numbers of patients he has to deal with. A working relationship with the epidemiologists is necessary, and with the social scientists as well; Talcott Parsons' work is a striking example of the latter. The authors have organized a scheme to screen a geographically defined part of the 'normal' population (Wellesly, a suburb of Boston, Mass.) for the presence of vulnerable individuals and to start preventive work on these. Thomas French's, Ives Hendrick's, and Robert Waelder's writings made the authors focus their work on the age group of four and a half to sixteen years. The paper deals with the assessment of 'a normal population as complementary to the type of clinical observations' which the authors so far have been unable to make. The project aims at prevention by tracing the formation of neurotic patterns as early as possible; by mobilizing the co-operation of the community as a whole the formation of fixed neurotic responses is counteracted. Thus valuable constructive assistance can be given at various age levels both for the family, the children, the schools, and the community.

282

responseSet.title: Revue Française De Psychanalyse, 15, 1951, No. 2: A. Hesnard.  'Evolution of the Idea of the Superego in Psycho-analytic Theory.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Revue Française De Psychanalyse, 15, 1951, No. 2

A. Hesnard. 'Evolution of the Idea of the Superego in Psycho-analytic Theory.'

F. Edwards

F. Edwards

F. Edwards  

Summarizing his new outlook on the superego the author states that two distinct methods of scientific observation can be compared, yielding two separate conceptions of psychic life: (1) from the subjective angle—i.e. the world of subjective meanings and ethical values; (2) from the objective angle—i.e. in terms of behaviour as a dialectical series of adaptations.

The author concludes that guilt and prohibition are the primordinal facts which obviate the necessity for the 'absurdity' 'the unconscious sense of guilt'. The term superego can be abandoned and replaced by a particular arrangement of the ego as a unique entity, engaged in adaptation to inhibitions and prohibitions throughout the developmental process.

282

responseSet.title: Samiksa, 5, 1951, No. 3: Louis A. Schwartz.  'Psychodynamic Aspect of Peptic Ulcer.'
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Samiksa, 5, 1951, No. 3

Louis A. Schwartz. 'Psychodynamic Aspect of Peptic Ulcer.'

Gordon Levinson

Gordon Levinson

Gordon Levinson  

Some of the literature, both physiological and psychological, on the causation of peptic ulcer, is briefly reviewed. A research group of twenty-one patients is then described and statistically analysed.

Traits common to the group included loneliness, ambition, fear of rejection, strong humanitarian impulses, belief that emotional conflicts were the basis of their symptoms, vivid childhood memories, and strong family loyalties. Improvement in symptoms was commonly associated with depression, frequent dermatological disorders and bowel difficulties.

The actual work done with the patients is not discussed, but it appears that a great deal of material was obtained from them, from which a general formulation of the psychodynamic factors was made. The main findings were marked hostility to the mother arising out of a passive dependency, anxiety in males as to their passive homosexual attitudes, which in an intolerant cultural group may explain the higher incidence in males, and intense sibling rivalry.

282

responseSet.title: Textbook of Abnormal Psychology: By Roy M. Dorcus and G. Wilson Shaffer.  4th edition.  (London:  Baillière, Tindall & Cox.  Pp. 717.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Textbook of Abnormal Psychology

By Roy M. Dorcus and G. Wilson Shaffer. 4th edition. (London: Baillière, Tindall & Cox. Pp. 717.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

This book, written for psychologists, contains a mass of information. Inevitably, many items are incorrect and obsolete. Psycho-analysis gets a poor deal. None of Freud's writings published in English after 1920 has been mentioned. In referring to the psycho-analytical theory of obsessional neurosis the author quotes the seduction hypothesis dropped by Freud half a century ago. The bibliography comprises 1, 200 items, unfortunately not in alphabetical order.

There is no indication of the date of the appearance of this book or its previous editions, a most irritating feature which makes the intelligent reader feel that he is treated with contempt. There are two undated forewords and two prefaces. The book, with all its faults, is probably useful, but it raises the question whether psychologists in writing such textbooks are not taking on too much. It seems to be beyond the capacity of a single author, or even of two, to write a really good textbook of abnormal psychology.

336

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 195334338-339EThe Dynamics of MoralsBy Radhakamal Mukerjee. (London: Macmillan, 1952. Pp. 530. 25 s.)T.L.Rowley
responseSet.title: The Dynamics of Morals: By Radhakamal Mukerjee.  (London:  Macmillan, 1952.  Pp. 530.  25 s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Dynamics of Morals

By Radhakamal Mukerjee. (London: Macmillan, 1952. Pp. 530. 25 s.)

T. Rowley

T. Rowley

T. Rowley  

In this book the author attempts to establish a rational ethical theory of human culture, using his vast knowledge of both Eastern and Western habits, and postulating rational approaches to future ethics.

338

His early statement that the psycho-analytic concept of the superego in the individual is too narrow and leaves out too much man's conscious ability to manipulate his unconscious and his use of rationality indicate clearly to the psycho-analytically orientated what to expect; and indeed what the book amounts to is an excellent account of how inter-group anxieties can be handled and behaviour become more controllable. The analyst, however, interested as he is in sociological problems, will feel dissatisfied with the building of hopes for improvement in future inter-group relationships when such hopes are based on so-called rational approaches. In his day-to-day contacts with, for example, the intellectual obsessional he unfortunately knows too well that what is known to be reasonable, and what it is obvious should be done, can nevertheless often not be done, owing to deep unconscious conflicts expressing themselves in contrary fashion.

No, for groups as well as for the individual, what is to be aimed at is an equilibrium where anxieties are resolved and understood, so that behaviour can be determined without unconscious distortion of reality—without recourse to high-sounding plausible rational systematizations which unfortunately are nearly always only poor compromises and reassurances against persistent unconscious anxiety-ridden conflicts.

339

responseSet.title: Dynamic Psychiatry: Edited by Franz Alexander and Helen Ross.  (Chicago:  University of Chicago Press, & London:  Cambridge University Press, 1952.  Pp. 578.  75s.)
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Dynamic Psychiatry

Edited by Franz Alexander and Helen Ross. (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, & London: Cambridge University Press, 1952. Pp. 578. 75s.)

E. Stengel

E. Stengel

E. Stengel  

In this important book a number of leading American psychiatrists have set out to examine the impact of psycho-analysis on psychiatry and allied sciences. Most of them are clinicians who have

341

had many opportunities to test the value of psycho-analytic theory outside the private consulting room. By dynamic psychiatry they understand psychiatry influenced by psycho-analysis. In Alexander's view the advent of the dynamic orientation in psychiatry has brought the isolated existence of psycho-analysis to an end. Most of the contributors are catholic in their views and tend to combine the psycho-analytic with a comprehensive psychiatric approach. The potentialities of psycho-analysis in the elucidation and treatment of the psychoses remain controversial. Professor Whitehorn of Baltimore believes that in this field some mutually comprehensible modus vivendi between dynamic and organic psychiatry needs to be worked out. Professor Levine of Cincinnati discusses the considerations on which treatment of mental disorders has to be based: (1) Clinical diagnosis, i.e. a formulation of the clinical categories to which the patient's reaction belongs; (2) Dynamic diagnosis, i.e. the assessment of the internal and external forces operating in the production of the patient's difficulties; (3) Genetic diagnosis, referring to the origin and development of the internal forces which are in small part hereditary but are predominantly based on early life experiences; (4) Transference and counter-transference. The dynamic approach is not incompatible with the use of physical treatments in mental disorder. It forms the basis of the 'psycho-somatic approach' which is covered by Alexander with his customary lucidity. An excellent chapter by Professor Brosin of Pittsburg deals with the contributions of psycho-analysis to the study of organic cerebral disorders. The same author reviews the psycho-analytic work on the psychoses. Other contributors are Thomas French, Edoardo Weiss, Margaret Gerard, Leon Saul, Louis Shapiro, John Lyons, Thomas Szasz. In the final part of the book Margaret Mead discusses some relationships between social anthropology and psycho-analysis. David Shakow contributes a chapter on clinical psychology. Professor David Levy writes on animal psychology in its relation to psychiatry. The book concludes with a valuable review by Professor Brosin on the influence of psycho-analysis on current thought. The book has been edited with great care. Each chapter has an excellent bibliography. This is one of the few books of which it can be said that it satisfies a real need. Teachers and students of psychiatry alike will find it an extremely valuable guide. The research worker, too, will be well advised to consult this book, which is the best and most authoritative presentation of the dynamic trend in psychiatry so far published.

342

[[RunningHead]]

Philosophy and Psycho-Analysis

By John Wisdom. (Oxford: Basil Blackwell, 1953. Pp. 282. 22 s. 6 d.)

Allan Strauss

Allan Strauss

Allan Strauss  

Problems in philosophy often appear to be wholly distinct from those of either empirical science or pure logic, for they are generally found to be insoluble by any observational or deductive procedure. Thus has arisen the widespread yet oft-disputed belief that they are not in themselves genuine problems at all, but are really symptoms of an underlying linguistic confusion, and therefore can be eliminated by unearthing the nature and sources of this confusion. In Philosophy and Psycho-analysis Professor Wisdom brings together some of his writings of the past twenty years in which he has made distinctive contributions towards clarifying this issue. The more technical articles early in the book are over-condensed and difficult to follow, but others (especially the extensive final chapter)
